title: Transient superionic state in ultrafast-irradiated post-transition metal oxides
authors: N. Medvedev, N. Nikishev, A. Artímez Peña
abstract: Matter under irradiation may enter unusual transient states, outside of its equilibrium phase diagram. One of such states is a superionic-like state, in which one sublattice of a compound liquifies, whereas another one remains solid. Here, we study theoretically post-transition metal oxides under ultrafast excitation of its electronic system, identifying which compounds produce such a superionic state. It is shown that oxides with sufficiently sparce metallic sublattices (e.g. corundum structure) generally form transient superionic states via nonthermal phase transition. More closely packed lattices (such as the zinc-blend structure in ZnO and CdO) do not exhibit superionicity. Tl and Pb oxides only enter thermally-produced superionic states (induced by the atomic heating via electron-phonon coupling), but not nonthermal ones. Sn and Bi oxides demonstrate states that cannot be clearly classified, in which oxygen subsystem diffuses significantly more and faster than the metallic one, but the metallic one is not stable as it would be in a truly superionic state.
summary: This paper investigates how ultrafast laser irradiation can drive metal oxides into transient superionic states where only one atomic sublattice melts. It establishes that the crystal structure (sparse vs. dense) is a primary predictor of whether a material undergoes superionic transition or complete melting.
main_problem: Identifying which post-transition metal oxides produce transient superionic states (where one sublattice liquefies while the other remains solid) under ultrafast irradiation and understanding the mechanisms (nonthermal vs. thermal) driving these transitions.
main_result: The study finds that oxides with sparse metallic sublattices (corundum structure) generally form transient superionic states via nonthermal phase transitions, whereas closely packed lattices (zinc-blende) undergo nonthermal melting.
method: Multiscale simulations using the XTANT-3 code, combining Transport Monte Carlo for electron dynamics, the Boltzmann Equation for electron scattering, and Molecular Dynamics for atomic motion.
model_or_system: A variety of post-transition metal oxides (including Al2O3, Ga2O3, In2O3, ZnO, CdO, etc.) subjected to 92 eV photon energy pulses simulating FLASH free-electron laser irradiation.
key_observables: Element-resolved mean displacement (to distinguish superionicity from melting), damage threshold in eV/atom, and transition type (nonthermal vs. thermal).
important_parameters: Photon energy (92 eV), pulse duration (10 fs), electronic and atomic temperatures, and lattice structure (corundum vs. zinc-blende).
main_assumptions: Uses a relaxation time approximation for electron-electron interaction and a transferable tight-binding method for interatomic potentials.
figures_summary: The notes mention Figure 1 shows mean atomic displacement, likely used to identify the superionic and melting regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of transient states, describes the XTANT-3 multiscale modeling framework, presents results categorized by crystal structure and transition mechanism, and concludes with a classification of different oxide behaviors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2. Electronic and atomic (average and element-specific) temperatures in (a) In2O3 in irradiated with the dose 2 eV/atom; (b) Tl2O3 irradiated with 0.7 eV/atom; (c) PbO irradiated with 0.2 eV/atom; (d) ZnO irradiated

Figure 2 shows that the partial temperatures of different elements are almost the same during the entire phase transition and after it in all cases, except for ZnO where Zn temperature slightly overshoots oxygen temperature just after the energy deposition, at ~50 fs. This selective acceleration of one species indicates that they are more sensitive to modification of the interatomic potential induced by the electronic excitation, as was previously reported for some materials [36,38]. Their temperatures equilibrate after ~500 fs.
